speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. The interviewer is Amy Margolis. It's in New York, New York, United States. The interview is being conducted wholly in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I-- and what I said before, they used to wake us up 5 o'clock in the morning, gave us -- the hot water that keep you talk. And, you know, as I said again, in rags, dressed in rags. And shoes made from-- from car rubbers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tires.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tires, yes, from tires. And-- and during daytime, the snow used to melt, and-- and steam used to come out from my shoes. Steam, real. And when I came back to the-- to the-- to the barrack, I used to get out-- pour out-- pour about a quart of water. And my legs now, they are, you know, very black since then because of the temperature and of those conditions. But they are still OK, the legs. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but the-- and-- and they were-- oh, and they formed us in two. And a soldier with a-- with a rifle, and used to say-- I believe I've said this before, no? Didn't I? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? And-- and you didn't-- you didn't have-- you got to ask the man to go-- let's say you have some cramps. You have-- but otherwise, if you took one step to the left or to the right, he had the right to shoot you. And even I remember then there came a commission to our-- our barrack. And you would ask our-- just our people to what party used to belong. Some of them who were communist-- used to go-- used to belong to the Communist Party, they would say, yes, I used to be a communist. Some of them would say, I was-- I used to belong to the Bund, which is a socialist. In my case, I-- I didn't belong to any party. And I-- and I said I did not belong. It took us maybe two, three months. It came a verdict, you know, from Moscow or from where. Those people who said they are communist, they gave them a-- a verdict for-- for 10 years to-- to serve in those-- in those camps. Those who said they belong-- belonged to the Socialist Party, they used to get five years. And those who-- like myself who said they not belong to any party, three years. They called them social [NON-ENGLISH], which means very dangerous element. Yeah. Yeah. And this went through, we used-- people used to die. And what-- and dying was this-- it started with the legs. They used to get swollen legs because of-- of the no-- no nutrition, nothing. And soon-- and at-- at-- even if you used to talk to that guy, everything was OK. As soon the swelling came through the body, without no pain, they died. In the morning, used to try to wake him up, nobody to talk to. He was dead. Then we used to have a big problem to bury. And it was-- the-- the-- the ground was ice. Then we could dig, let's say, five, 10 inches. And we used to put down woods. They used to burn for 24 hours. The next day, we could dig another 10 inches. And as soon we got about, let's say, over a-- a foot and a half, we would put that guy into the grave, because he-- he was just a piece of ice. And I would say, in my-- in the place where I used to be, which was close to Uchta, the-- the town, not far from Uchta-- U-C-H-T-A-- I would say in my estimation about 40% in that short time, 14 months, died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it all men?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: All men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men, just men, at the age, let's say, from, let's say, 17 through at most 25. Innocent boys, some of them very educated, some less educated, big town, small towns. But funny thing, we-- I did not realize-- nobody realized-- the whole fight was how to survive, how to get a piece of bread which you couldn't get from no place. Because this was a place where, in one way, they used to take those people, those criminals-- I call them criminals. They are not criminals. One way. The other way, they used to take all those woods, measured without the branches, and-- to the Soviet Union. You couldn't run away. It was no place where to run. All the place, just snow and wood. No towns, nothing. You couldn't buy anything. You didn't have any money. No stores. Constantly hungry and constantly fighting for survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No place where-- it was no place where to escape, you understand? It was a territory in the hundreds of miles, just wood and snow. And no even villages, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just camps and camps. I found out that about the next camp was-- which he was sentenced for 10 years, big shot in the Soviet-- Radek. Most of them used to get death sentence. But this guy used to get 10 years. And he was not far from our-- from our concentration camp. And what happened then, the war broke out between the Soviet Union and the German government in 1941. I believe this was in June or in July. And the American government and the Soviet Union became allies. Became allies, and they made an agreement for those people like we were, those-- those refugees, to let them free, to get them out of the-- of those-- of those concentration camps. And it took them-- the list, they used to-- took a few days. And a friend of mine, his name was called. My name, not yet. And I was constantly waiting. And I-- I was the unlucky one that-- and I was on the last list. And finally, OK, I am-- I am getting out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know what it meant, what these lists meant? How did you know where you would be going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this list came because they had a list of-- of-- of our people. They came from Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you not afraid that they were-- would put you in even worse conditions somehow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. You see, what I'm saying again. The war broke out between the Soviet Union and the German government in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't learn. But we found out that-- we-- we were told that was a war, and we-- we going to get free.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Soviets told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- yes, yes. They told us that we going to get free. And-- and the lists were coming not complete, just partially. Some day a few-- five people, 10 people, 20 people. And I was on the-- took a few, maybe two weeks, and I was on the list. And then again, we got out of that-- of that camp, and got into the-- the warmer weather, into-- more to the south, the south of Soviet. Also in very bad conditions, and all the times they-- but we were free people at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I remember, also, among us were real Polacks. Not Jew. We were Jews, Polish Jews. Then we found out that-- at that time, they wouldn't say anything. But when we got free, they used to call this one captain, this lieutenant, this colonel. You know? We would never realize that those guys, those Polish guys who were with us also, they are former Polish officers, and high-ranking officers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were also told that we going to go to the Polish Army in Iran. Because over there used to be the Polish General Anders, who used to form a Polish army against Hitler. And what happened, that on the middle of the way going to Iran, they stop-- they just took the Polish, the real Polacks. They went to Iran. And the Jewish guys they left back in the Soviet Union. Some Jews got away, like the very well-known Israeli Prime Minister Menachem Begin. He was also in one of those camps. And he, I don't know, he was-- listen, this was Begin-- and-- and he got into Iran, and from Iran he got to Palestine at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they transporting you from place to place?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they transporting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thankfully, again, it was, of course, it-- at that-- they were trains, also in bad condition, but not locked completely. We were-- those also--

speaker: interviewer
text: The trains again?

speaker: interviewee
text: --merchandise cars, but-- but not locked. They would give us more food, you know. And they tried to get some-- some better-- better clothing. But it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally what happened, the Polacks, they went to Iran. And the Jewish guys, in my case, they sent us to the-- to Asia. At the very, very well-known river, a tremendous river-- Amu-Daria-- A-M-U dash D-A-R-I-A-- River, maybe two, three miles, the width. Such a tremendous river. And over there, it-- they used to have those-- the-- they used to grow-- the-- some shirts are made from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cotton?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cotton, yeah, cotton. And we arrived there. And finally, they-- they were on some very poor conditions. The-- even the population used to live on so very simple. But they used to-- the-- the government over-- the local government would give us 400 gram flour, and that's it. And what happened, at that-- over there it was a fishing kolkhoz, which means-- and they used to throw out the-- the inside from the fish. And they just, with salt, covered the fish. And we-- they used to throw out those-- those-- and we used to mix it, the flour with those insides from the-- from the fish, and with water, without any-- even no salt. Nothing. And that was our food for a-- for a while, being-- as soon the Soviet Union used to go more to the-- to the west. They used to take us, again, and-- and to the little-- also to the north. And then I found out-- I came, they got me into a little town of-- close to the-- the river. And meantime, I learned a little-- when you young, you learn fast-- and I learned a little bit Russian. And they saw that I knew better than others. They took me to an office. Office. And I became a bookkeeper at that office. They gave me a little bit more clothings, better clothings. But-- and-- and we used to live about 40 people in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you a bookkeeper for? What kind of organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the Soviet-- the Soviet-- the Soviet government--

speaker: interviewer
text: The government.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who used to organize this. Yeah. And at that time, I remember they-- they formed in the Soviet Union Organization of Polish Patriots. And the-- the top lady was a writer, Polish writer, Wanda Wasilewska. The-- the first name is W-A-N-D-A. And the last name is W-A-S-I-L-E-W-S-K-A, Wasilewska. And at that time, you know, they used to-- in those-- used to get some products from the-- the United States to support us. And I remember I used to get some shoes, military shoes, and it was a big deal for me. I got military shoes. I got some blankets. And from a blanket, I made a-- a suit. From two blankets, I believe. Yeah, I believe. From two blankets. It was not a suit, it was something like the military, you know, without, the-- just the pants. And this was, at that-- it was the good-- the good-- at that time, I used to get 800 gram bread, which means about 2 pounds of bread. And bread was the main-- the main product. And I used to get some soup. I was not hungry, but very, very primitive. And I used to work. I used to be in that office until again they said-- the-- the Soviet Army used to go again more-- more to the west. Again, we went to-- to Zaporozh'e. That-- that's the-- that's the eastern part-- the-- the western part of the Soviet Union. And-- and then again, I was in-- in the-- working in the office as a bookkeeper. At that time, I learned better the-- the Russian language, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we just back up for a few minutes?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's back up for a few minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I wish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to Komi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I am jumping.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're not jumping, but I just want a little bit more detail--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about Komi.

speaker: interviewee
text: About Komi?

speaker: interviewer
text: Komi. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was there any medical attention given?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only attention was that they have just a thermostat, a thermometer, we call it, thermostat. If you have at least 38, which means on the-- on your-- on your, it will be about 103 or 104, you've got to go to work. If you didn't-- you didn't have-- the regular is, I believe, 99, right? Normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: About, about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then it means that you got-- you got to have 104, or 103. I don't remember exactly. See, so many years back. They-- they wouldn't-- you have to go to work. I remember, I used to get-- from malnutrition, I used to get here a-- a wound. Because the body used up all the-- all the reserves. And I start to limp. And I couldn't walk, even in Komi. Then the guy with that-- with that-- with that-- how you--

speaker: interviewer
text: The rifle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rifle, used to push me. Hit me. Go, go, go, go. And I couldn't. I limped. In such conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if somebody developed a fever over 103 or 104--

speaker: interviewee
text: They got to go to work. And people-- oh yes, and I-- and that's what I said before. People used to die from malnutrition. First, they used to-- as I told before, they used to get swollen legs. Soon this came through the body. They died without any-- any pain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a medical center that the very sick ones were brought to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No medical center. It was just a guy who used to look at you. You know, no-- he was not a-- not a doctor, even not-- not even a nurse. Just he was the lucky guy, they let him be like a nurse. But he was not a nurse even.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No medicine. No. No medicine, no-- no doctors, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he looked at you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: You would-- listen, as I told you. We got to work seven days a week, and the horses six days a week. Which means you were just a number and just a-- an animal. Not a-- everything was un-human conditions. It's unbelievable. You see, if my English would be better, if this was my-- my native language, I would have explained it-- if I could say this in-- everything in Polish or in Yiddish, it would sound completely different, and worse. Because-- because treated people like animals. Un-human conditions. And I wouldn't say that just to us, everybody. Even those Russians, they suffered, those-- those people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they shoot people on the spot, like the Nazis did?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, what once happened that one of our people, he was out of shape, completely. And what happened, when we went out, they made signs where we can walk. If you go out of this place, he-- that guy got the right to shoot to you. And one-- one of our guys, he was out of-- he was completely out. You know, like the Germans would say Muselmann. But we used to say [YIDDISH]. And he-- he didn't know he crossed that sign. And all of a sudden we hear-- and he went, you know, to-- just to take care, you know, to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden we hear shooting. And what happened? That guy shoot this guy. We brought him back to the-- to the-- to the barrack, with-- carry him. Then the main man of the barrack, the-- the-- the manager, he shook hands with that-- with that guy because he think-- he was thinking that he was running away. He want escape. It was no place to escape. Were there different roles that the prisoners were made to play in the barracks? Who was the manager?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barrack? No manager. We didn't have-- the manager was one guy, Russian guy, who was also a criminal. But not-- and he was the top of that-- he was-- this was his penalty, that they send him over there to Komi and to take care on a camp. But he was also a criminal. The top man, he was a criminal, but not such bad sentence. He used to have steak. He used to have more bread, more-- more-- more food. That's the only man who was in charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a kitchen. Kitchen, the man who used to cook, just the soup and the fish. I never used to get a piece of fish because I couldn't do the-- the way they would like me to produce. Who could produce? Some could. The Russians, they could.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our people couldn't produce. That's the reason they-- they died. They-- you know, they couldn't-- you see, what happened also, once in a while they would give you a little bit-- let's say, four, five ounces of sugar. That's only once a month. That's the only thing additional that-- what we used to get.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the more work you did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the more you were rewarded with more food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- no. If you did more-- more than normal, the 100%, according to them, then you would get 800 gram bread, and-- and the soup, and a-- a-- a piece of-- of-- of salted fish. They didn't have the-- those frozen, salted fish. Because of that, you didn't need a refrigerator. You know? The whole-- the whole place was one-- one refrigerator.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody in the barracks ever practice any kind of religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would-- I remember even in Bialystok, when we were in the church, some of them stood up and prayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about Komi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Commence?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Komi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Komi? Also some. Oh, yes. This what reminds me. Among us was one rabbi from Hungary. And as I told you, we used to work seven days a week. And over there, it was this way, that it was no wind. Because if-- where-- the-- if we-- you would have wind, you would able to be alive. Quiet, completely, no nothing. And this rabbi, on a Saturday he didn't want to work. But the problem was there this way. If you moved a little bit, you worked. Nothing would happen. But-- but he was standing up. And what happened, that he got all the fingers. They cut all the fingers at the hands and the legs. And of course, gangrene, you know-- know, infection. And he died because he didn't-- he didn't-- he stood up by his belief. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he didn't work--

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: He wasn't moving, and he got frostbite.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on Saturday. No, he didn't-- he didn't want to work on Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he got frostbite because he was still.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. That's correct. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it's so many years back, you know. And especially in that kind of condition. Normally, you know, after that, I would think I-- I missed a lot of other things, which don't come-- come to me right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why I'm asking you questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: But so many bad things. It's unbelievable, unbelievable. And some would cry about parents-- you know, they missed the parents. But most of the people were thinking how to put something into their mouth. How to survive. That's what's kept those young people alive, just to be alive. Maybe tomorrow will be a better day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the conversation, typically, in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, we didn't have any chances to-- to conversation. Because first of all, you came back from that-- from the forest, you want to lay down. You were so deadly tired that-- and, you know, you wanted to rest. And-- and we came back, it was maybe 8 o'clock in the-- at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they used to wake you up at 5 o'clock in the morning. And I recall very little conversation. The only conversation is about how to survive, how to get something, how to steal or anything. You know? And you were afraid even to leave a little piece, because if you would leave-- if you would leave it, another fellow would steal it. And-- not because he was a-- he would like to steal it, but you know, the stomach was empty. What-- what basically was the attitude toward each other among the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friendly, very friendly, good. Good, very good, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we knew we are in the same shoes. All of-- the same, in the same conditions, the same chances, and in no-- or no chances at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to converse while you were working, or were you not allowed to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- did you try to converse, to talk, while you were working, or were you not allowed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't. First of all, you were afraid to talk. You see, in the Soviet Union, the best way to survive to kept silent, not to talk at all. Because otherwise you can get-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If you talk, you can get-- get into trouble. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- we learned immediately the best way, not to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any attempted rebellions in Komi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, rebellion. Just the Russians. The Russians, yeah. I remember once, it was a rebellion. They used to took away those-- the-- those rifles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And it was a big fight. And finally, they lost, those Russians. And what happened next to them, I don't know. Those-- the real criminals who used to kill people or used to murder-- yeah, it was. It was. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: They rebelled, the criminals rebelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. Yes? OK. I believe now it was a little better, no? Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- when the criminals tried to rebel--

speaker: interviewee
text: I see.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did they steal rifles from the soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot tell you. But I remember it was shooting, and we used to talk about how this happened, I don't know. We were-- we didn't want to mix in-- in anything. We-- we were afraid, young, want to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your thoughts while you were in those barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself? I would be honest to you. I don't-- at this moment, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I wanted to survive. I remember I had a mother, father, brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you thought about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go back now after you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We-- no. Then-- well, let's see. Then we were in Zaporozh'e and in that sovkhoz. And finally, they said we going back-- back to Poland. And how it happened. Yeah, I remember. My top bookkeeper wished me very well. He said to me, you were living in very bad conditions. We were living also in bad conditions, but you going back. But he was very careful, because this was the last day before-- before we left. And in Soviet Union, you don't talk too much because you can get in big trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this guy used to like me. I used to help him a lot. And he used to say, you know, you are in-- in not good condition, 40 people in one room. You know? And what kind of a living? We at least, a whole family in one room, but at least.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but at least you're going back to Poland. They didn't realize that Poland is going to be communist. But you going back to a free country. And we are going to stay here, and we had problems and trouble, and-- and-- and still we going to live with those problems. And you going-- you suffered a lot, he said to me. But at least you're going back to your family, to a free country. But we are not in a free country. We suffered. And he said to me-- he was very careful. He looked around, there's nobody here-- and-- and he had some trust. He believe me that I won't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: He took a big chance by trusting somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: He took a big chance by trusting somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he took a chance. He took a chance, yes. But he was not a big talker anyway. But he was jealous, and probably. Not jealous, but, you know, those people are tired of the way they live. It's unbelievable, how they live. I remember over there, it was the good life. We used to get food in that-- in-- in that-- not a restaurant, it's a such kitchen. Every day, the same thing. Every day. The kasha, and-- and the kapusta. How is kapusta. No. Kapusta, which means-- how to say-- get-- I know this word, but it got out of me. The same thing every day. The same thing, you know. No meat. No meat, just a little tomato soup on that kasha, and a little soup. And that's-- that's for-- for dinner. And in the--
